food_name: Spicy Beef Tripe Salad (Fu Qi Fei Pian)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Fu Qi Fei Pian / Spicy Beef Tripe Salad. Mapped to broader 'beef dish' class (FOODON_03315595) as the closest defensible anchor. A more specific offal salad class does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for cold Sichuan beef offal salad. A00LC (Beef meat products, cooked) is the closest defensible broader category. Offal-specific cold-mixed dish codes are absent from FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for Fu Qi Fei Pian or Sichuan cold beef offal salad as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Spicy Beef Tripe Salad is approximately 150 g, typically presented as a cold appetizer plate at a Sichuan restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, variety meats and by-products, tripe, cooked, simmered (FDC ID 174033) and Beef, variety meats and by-products, heart, cooked, simmered (FDC ID 174034); composite estimate adjusted for chili oil dressing
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for the complete Fu Qi Fei Pian dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked beef tripe (FDC ID 174033), cooked beef heart (FDC ID 174034), and cooked beef tongue (FDC ID 174036), weighted approximately 60% tripe / 25% heart / 15% tongue, then adjusted upward for fat and sodium to account for the Sichuan chili oil dressing and soy sauce. Input file values (energy 185 kcal, protein 12.5 g, fat 14.2 g, carbohydrate 2.5 g, sodium 480 mg) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. Saturated fat estimated at 3.8 g per 100 g based on USDA fatty acid profiles for cooked beef offal plus chili oil contribution.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of High-Protein, High-Fat, Low-Carbohydrate Mixed Dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked beef and offal composite; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Fu Qi Fei Pian in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 15 (low) based on the analogous category of high-protein, high-fat, very-low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes. Cooked beef and offal have negligible starch content; the primary carbohydrate contributors are soy sauce and sugar in the dressing (~2.5 g per 100 g total). The glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 2.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.56, rounded to 0.6. This is consistent with the low-GI classification for protein-dominant dishes documented in Atkinson et al. 2008.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (480 mg per 100 g; ~720 mg per 150 g serving) from soy sauce and seasoned brine
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load; if consumed, portion size should be restricted to no more than 75 g and the chili oil dressing should be reduced or omitted.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.5 g per 100 g) and high protein (12.5 g per 100 g) supporting glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its very low carbohydrate and glycemic load; however, the high fat content from chili oil should be factored into total daily energy intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated total fat (14.2 g per 100 g) including saturated fat (~3.8 g per 100 g) from beef offal and chili oil, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally and in small portions, given the combined burden of saturated fat, total fat, and high sodium, all of which are risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Beef Tripe Salad, known in Mandarin as Fu Qi Fei Pian (夫妻肺片, literally 'Husband and Wife Lung Slices'), is a celebrated cold appetizer originating from Chengdu, Sichuan Province, China. Despite its name, the dish does not typically contain lung; it is made from thinly sliced beef tripe, heart, and tongue that are first simmered in a fragrant spiced brine until tender, then chilled and sliced paper-thin. The sliced offal is dressed in a complex, fiery sauce combining Sichuan chili oil, Chinkiang black vinegar, soy sauce, sugar, and ground Sichuan peppercorns, then garnished with crushed peanuts, toasted sesame seeds, and fresh coriander. The dish is renowned for its 'ma-la' (麻辣) flavor profile — a simultaneous numbing sensation from Sichuan peppercorns and intense heat from chili oil — balanced by savory, sweet, and tangy notes. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 185 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 14.2 g fat, and only 2.5 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, high-fat, very-low-carbohydrate dish. Sodium is elevated at 480 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce and brine seasoning. It is a good source of iron (2.2 mg), zinc (1.8 mg), vitamin B12 (1.2 mcg), and niacin (3.5 mg) from the beef offal. Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its negligible glycemic impact, but should be limited by individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium and fat content. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with soy, peanut, or sesame allergies.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for the complete Fu Qi Fei Pian dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central composite estimates for cooked beef tripe, heart, and tongue, adjusted for the chili oil dressing and soy sauce. The input file values are consistent with this composite and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on the low-carbohydrate, high-protein meat dish category from the University of Sydney GI Database reference literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans), cholesterol, and moisture data for this specific composite Sichuan cold beef offal dish are not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central for the assembled dish as a whole; while individual offal entries (FDC IDs 174033, 174034, 174036) contain some fatty acid data, the chili oil dressing contribution makes a reliable composite unavailable without laboratory analysis of the finished dish. These five fields are therefore set to -1 per the negative sentinel value policy, indicating data not available for this composite preparation. Saturated fat estimated at 3.8 g per 100 g based on USDA fatty acid profiles for cooked beef tripe and heart plus estimated chili oil contribution; this is an approximation. The input source 'Sichuan Academy of Agricultural Sciences' (http://www.saas.sh.cn) is not on the approved source list and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the primary nutrient evidence base. GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Fu Qi Fei Pian was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. The input aliases contained a Cantonese-style romanization 'Fu1 Cai1 Fai3 Pin2' which appears to be an error; the correct Mandarin pinyin is Fūqī Fèipiàn. The erroneous form has been replaced with the corrected Mandarin romanization 'Fu1 Qi1 Fei4 Pian4'. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical 150 g entries) has been deduplicated to a single 150 g serving. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains beef offal and the slaughter certification cannot be confirmed for a generic recipe record.
